coredata.dc:description: Zinc is an essential micronutrient. It has significant roles in innumerable biological processes and enzymatic reactions that occur in human body. Deficiency of zinc causes acrodermatitis enteropathica and excess consumption of zinc is responsible for diabetes, cerebral ischemia, epilepsy, neural degeneration, and neurological disorders like Alzheimer’s disease and Parkinson’s disease. Therefore, monitoring of level of zinc in body fluid and tissue may be effective in early detection of diseases. In recent years, cell permeable fluorescent dye-based probes have been reported for sensing intracellular Zn2+ ions but there is no report on development of carbon quantum dot (CQD) for sensing Zn2+ in plasma. In this study CQD of ∼1 nm size was synthesized form onion extract using hydrothermal method. They were quasi-spherical in shape and had lattice stripes due to graphitic core. Particle surface was decorated with polyhydroxy group and amino group. When excited at 325 nm, the presence of Zn2+ ions significantly enhanced the fluorescence intensity in Tris buffer and blood plasma, whereas others common cations like Fe3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, and Hg2+ ions had similar effect in quenching the fluorescence intensity. The quantum yield was 6.214 % and was very much sensitive to the presence of Zn2+ ions. The limit of detection and limit of quantitation values for Zn2+ ions in plasma were 6.4 μM and 21.3 μM respectively. The affinity constant of Zn2+ to CQD was 600 M−1 whereas the association constant was 185 M−1.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis of carbon quantum dot from crushed onion.

Particle size distribution (A) and ζ-potential (B) of carbon quantum dot (CQD) as measured with Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) technique. The average particle size was ∼1 nm with a surface charge of -10.4 mV.

High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) analysis of CQD. A: Image under microscope (yellow arrows are used to mark the particles), B: Image of single CQD with lattice stripes at high resolution. The lattice stripes obtained after analysis (Fast Fourier Transform and Inverse FFT) of the image with Gatan Microscopy Suite Software is shown as ‘inset’. C: The line profile of lattice stripe. The lattice d-spacing was found to be 0.247 nm that corresponds to the lattice fringes of graphene. D: Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) pattern of CQD. E: Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum of CQD.

Structural investigation of CQD on the basis of optical energy bandgap. A: UV absorption spectrum of CQD. The absorbance maximum was 284 nm with optical density (OD) close to 1. Deconvolution was done to separate out the overlapping peaks. The graph obtained after deconvoulation is shown as ‘inset’. There was a peak at 325 nm with OD value ∼0.5. B: Calculation of optical energy bandgap by ‘Tauc plot’. Two energy bandgaps at 3.96 eV and 5.25 eV were obtained. C: Predicted structure of CQD as obtained by DFT simulation. D: Surface mapping of CQD on the basis of electrostatic potential (red: non-polar; blue-white: polar). So, the nanoparticle was hydrophilic in nature. E: The schematic representation of structures with HOMO, LUMO distribution (as obtained with DFT simulation) corresponding to the energy bandgap of ∼4 eV and ∼5.2 eV.

Investigation of emission properties (fluorescence) of CQD. A: Emission spectrum obtained after excitation with radiations of different wavelength. B: Contour plot. Excitation wavelength and emission maxima were plotted at X-axis and Y-axis respectively. Emission intensity at the emission maxima was plotted at Z-axis. Contour lines corresponding to different emission intensities are labelled. C: Fluorescence emission maxima obtained after exciting the CQD with radiations of different wavelength are plotted. D: The maximum emission intensities obtained after exciting the CQD with radiations of different wavelength are plotted.
Estimation of Quantum Yield of CQD with respect to Quinine sulphate. The excitation wavelength was 325 nm.

Sensing of metal ions with CQD. A: Effect of different metal ions (μM) on the fluorescence spectrum of CQD in Tris buffer (pH 7.4). B: Effect of different concentration of Zn2+ ion on the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQD in Tris buffer (pH 7.4). C: Effect of different concentration of Zn2+ ion on the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQD in the plasma of chicken blood. D: Plot of % enhancement in fluorescence intensity of CQD in Tris buffer (pH 7.4) (450 nm) in presence of different concentration of Zn2+ ions. E: Plot of % enhancement of fluorescence intensity of CQD in chicken blood plasma (518 nm) in presence of different concentration of Zn2+ ions. The plot upto 100 μM is shown as ‘inset’ in figure D and E. The slit width for photoluminescent measurements in Tris buffer and plasma was 5 nm and 2.5 nm respectively.

Estimation of affinity constant and association constant of CQD and Zn2+ ion interaction. Stern–Volmer plot (A and B) and Benesi–Hildebrand plot (C and D). A and C represent data for interaction in Tris buffer. B and D represent data for interaction in plasma. Error bar represent standard deviation. Experiments are replicated thrice.
FTIR spectra of CQD, Znic chloride and CQD-[Zn2+] complex.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots prepared from onion extract as fluorescence turn-on probes for selective estimation of Zn2+ in blood plasma

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, Guru Nanak Institute of Pharmaceutical Science & Technology, 157/F, Nilgunj Road, Panihati, Kolkata, 700114, West Bengal, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zinc is an essential micronutrient. It has significant roles in innumerable biological processes and enzymatic reactions that occur in human body. Deficiency of zinc causes acrodermatitis enteropathica and excess consumption of zinc is responsible for diabetes, cerebral ischemia, epilepsy, neural degeneration, and neurological disorders like Alzheimer’s disease and Parkinson’s disease. Therefore, monitoring of level of zinc in body fluid and tissue may be effective in early detection of diseases. In recent years, cell permeable fluorescent dye-based probes have been reported for sensing intracellular Zn2+ ions but there is no report on development of carbon quantum dot (CQD) for sensing Zn2+ in plasma. In this study CQD of ∼1 nm size was synthesized form onion extract using hydrothermal method. They were quasi-spherical in shape and had lattice stripes due to graphitic core. Particle surface was decorated with polyhydroxy group and amino group. When excited at 325 nm, the presence of Zn2+ ions significantly enhanced the fluorescence intensity in Tris buffer and blood plasma, whereas others common cations like Fe3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, and Hg2+ ions had similar effect in quenching the fluorescence intensity. The quantum yield was 6.214 % and was very much sensitive to the presence of Zn2+ ions. The limit of detection and limit of quantitation values for Zn2+ ions in plasma were 6.4 μM and 21.3 μM respectively. The affinity constant of Zn2+ to CQD was 600 M−1 whereas the association constant was 185 M−1.

ce:section.ce:para: Zinc is the 24th most abundant element on earth crust. In the periodic table zinc is the member of group IIb. Zinc is a micronutrient essential for innumerable biological processes and enzymatic reactions that occur in human body. It plays pivotal role in gene transcription, regulation of metalloenzymes, neural signal transmission and apoptosis [1,2]. Currently, the recommended dietary allowance (RDA) for zinc in the United States is 8 milligrams per day for women and 11 milligrams per day for men. In children, zinc plays important roles for mental and physical development [3]. It helps in preventing common cold, diarrhea; promotes wound healing and prevents age related macular degeneration [3]. Zinc is very much effective for fertility of women. Human body mainly contain 2–3 g m of zinc. 90 % of it is localized in bone and other organs like heart, kidney, prostate, pancreas, skin. The remaining amount is present in gastrointestinal tract. After oral administration, Zinc is absorbed mainly in small intestine. In plasma Zn binds with several proteins such as albumin, transferring, α-micro globulin. On the cellular level, 30–40 % of zinc is localized in the nucleus, 50 % in the cytosol and the remaining part is associated with membranes [4–6].

Deficiency of zinc causes acrodermatitis enteropathica and excess consumption of zinc is responsible for neurological disorders like Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s disease [1]. Taking up large doses of supplemental zinc over extended periods of time is frequently associated with copper deficiency leading to anemia [7]. The mechanism of copper deficiency induced anemia is based on the requirement of copper for several enzymes involved in iron transport and utilization and, therefore, in heme synthesis [8,9].

So, development a fluorescent probe for specific detection and quantification is of urgent need for diagnostic monitoring of zinc level in plasma and other organs. Different organic fluorophores have been developed for detecting Zn2+ invitro [1]. Though, a number of researchers have reported fluorescent probes for sensing intracellular Zn2+ ions [10–12], there is no report on development of probe for estimation of Zn2+ directly in plasma. Lippard et al. developed cell permeable fluorescent sensor based on fluorescein dye [10]. This sensor formed 1:1 complex with Zn2+ ions. This complex showed 3.1-fold increase in integrated fluorescence intensity. Wang et al. developed tetraphenylethylene (TPE)-peptide probe for sensing of intracellular Zn2+ [11]. The peptide moiety has three histidine residues which form self-assembling complex in presence of Zn2+ ion. This caused the TPE moieties in aggregated state leading to aggregation induced emission. Ren et al. developed N-(2-(bis(pyridine-2ylmethyl)amino)ethyl)-2-mercaptoacetamide (MDPA) modified gold nanoparticles as fluorescent and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) dual-mode probe for imaging of intracellular Zn2+ [12]. Recently Zhang et al. (2020) developed Zn2+ selective fluorescent sensor based on poly(2-oxazoline) (POx) cylindrical molecular brushes which had good water solubility and biocompatibility. This probe showed good cellular uptake and selectivity to Zn2+ ions [13]. So, all these reported probes were based on organic fluorophores that have many limitations in comparison to quantum dot [14]. Quantum dots typically have multi-exponential decay with fluorescence lifetime of 10−100 ns, whereas organic fluorophores show mono-exponential decay with fluorescence lifetime of 1−10 ns. The thermal stability and photochemical stability of quantum dots are much better than organic fluorophores [14].

For biological applications, carbon quantum dots are most suitable because of their biocompatibility, low toxicity and excellent fluorescent properties [15]. Actually carbon based different nanomaterials like carbon dot (CD), graphene quantum dot (GQD) are reported many biological applications like bio imaging and biosensing [16]. Microwave irradiation [17–19], ultrasonic passivation [20,21], arc discharge [22], hydrothermal treatment [23], combustion [24], oxidation, electrochemical exfoliation [25] and laser ablation [26–28] are strategies for synthesis of high-performance of CDs. Hydrothermal synthesis method has gained popularity because of it’s simplicity and avoidance of using sophisticated instruments. In addition, there are an abundance of precursors can be adopted to prepare CDs by hydrothermal method [29]. Many reports have been published where a vast variety of regularly consumed resources like organic chemicals, chemosynthetic polymers; natural polymers like gelatin, chitosan; bio waste materials such fruit peels, foods and drinks were used. This suitability of natural resources like banana [30], orange [31], onion [32], caneberry beans [33] makes this method promising for large scale synthesis. Depending on the condition of the reaction, the CD may be structurally and functionally different and may specifically sense the metal ions, such as Fe3+ [34–37], Hg2+ [38–40], Co2+ [41], Cu2+ [42] and Ag+ [43]. Guttena et al. synthesized CD using onion waste as precursor and they found it specific for detection of Fe3+ ions [32]. Though Zn2+ ion doped carbon dots have been reported, there is no report on development of carbon quantum dot for sensing of Zn2+ ions. Though Zhang.et.al developed carbon dot-based probe for detection of Zn2+ ions. Carbon dot was synthesized followed by coupling of quinoline derivative covalently on the CD surface. Actually, this quinoline derivative was the fluorophore responsible for detection of Zn2+ ion [44]. Though it was reported to have a detection limit as low as 6.4 nM in buffer, its suitability in blood plasma was not studied.

In this study, for the first time, we have reported the synthesis of carbon quantum dot as a ‘turn on’ sensor probe that can be used for quantitative estimation of Zn2+ ion in biological samples like plasma. It’s structure was investigated. Mechanism of fluorescence has been elucidated. Kinetics of interaction with CQD in buffer and plasma has been elucidated.

Ethylenediamine was purchased from Merck Life Science Private Limited, Vikhroli (East), Mumbai. Onion (Allium cepa) was collected from local market. Quinine sulphate was purchased from Hindustan Chemicals and Pharmaceuticals, Bhandup West. Lead nitrate was purchased from Indian Platinum Private Limited, Vile Parle West, Mumbai. Ferric chloride was purchased from Vinipul Inorganics Private Limited, Chembur East, and Mumbai. Zinc chloride was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific India Private Limited, Mumbai. Calcium chloride was purchased from Labdhi Chemicals, Mumbai West. Copper sulphate was purchased from Indian Platinum Private Limited,Vile Parle West, Mumbai. Mercury chloride was purchased from L.S. Chemicals & Pharmaceuticals, Andheri west, Mumbai. TRIS Buffer was purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories Private Limited, Maharashtra, India. Chicken blood was collected from local slaughter shop.

The scheme for method of synthesis is shown in Fig. 1 . 30 gm of finely crushed onion was boiled in 100 mL of double distilled for 30 min. The mixture was cooled down to room temperature and filtered through sintered glass filter. 0.6 mL EDA (ethylene diamine) was added in 20 mL filtrate and autoclaved for 150 min. A brown colored liquid was obtained. It was centrifuged at 5000 r.p.m for 15 min to remove any insoluble particles and subsequently filtered through 0.22 μm syringe filter. The filtrate was stored at 4 °C under darkness till further use [32]. It was diluted 500 times with Milli-Q water and referred as carbon quantum dot (CQD) throughout the manuscript.

UV–vis spectral analysis was recorded by Jasco-V670 manufactured by JASCO, USA. CQD was scanned within the range of 200 nm – 800 nm. The Spectra Manager® software was used for analysis of peaks after deconvolution [45].
Spectra were recorded with Jasco FP-8500 Spectro-fluorimeter (JASCO,USA) from 350 nm to 580 nm. [46] The excitation wavelength was 325 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of particle size distribution and ζ-potential
ce:section.ce:para: Dynamic light scattering technique was used for studying the particle size distribution of CQD. Particle size distribution (number average mean diameter and poly-dispersity index) and ζ-potential measurements were done with Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments Limited, U.K) at 25 °C, at a laser (633 nm) scattering angle of 90° [47].

One drop (10 μl) CQD was loaded on carbon-coated copper grid (300 mesh). After 60 s, the grid was dried by blotting the excess liquid with tissue paper. After further drying at 37 °C, the sample was investigated under a JEOL JEM 2100 h Transmission Electron Microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan). The images were analyzed with ImageJ software for calculation of average particle diameter.
The FTIR spectra were recorded with PerkinElmer UTAR Two. Background correction was done without sample. 5 μL liquid sample was placed on the diamond surface and the spectra were recorded [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural elucidation of CQD with density functional theory (DFT) simulation
ce:section.ce:para: Avogadro 1.1 software was used for generating the predicted structures of CQD [49]. Geometrical optimization & energy minimization operations were performed with Avogadro software using UFF (Universal Force Field) CHARMM force field [50,51]. We employed ab-initio DFT simulation for single-point energy calculation of HOMO-LUMO band gap [52–55]. DFT simulation operations were done by ORCA programming software 4.1 [56,57] in def2-SVP basic setup [58]. All the intrinsic molecular orbitals were visualized by IboView software, a program for chemical analysis [59,60].

The fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) is the ratio of photons absorbed to photons emitted through fluorescence. The most reliable method for recording ΦF is use of well characterized standard samples with known ΦF values. Essentially, solutions of the standard and test samples with identical absorbance at the same excitation wavelength can be assumed to be absorbing the same number of photons. Hence, a simple ratio of the integrated fluorescence intensities of the two solutions (recorded under identical conditions) will yield the ratio of the quantum yield value. We used quinine sulphate as the standard fluorescent sample [46,61].

The absorbance of a series of standard samples of CQD in Milli Q water and quinine sulphate in 0.5 N sulphuric acid were estimated at 325 nm. The fluorescence spectra of these samples were recorded after excitation at 325 nm. The optical density (OD) values of different dilutions of a sample were plotted against corresponding AUC values of fluorescence spectra. Linear regression analysis was done to calculate the slope (Grad) of the straight line.
Φx=Φst (Gradx /GradST)(η2 X / η2 ST)
Where the subscripts ST and X denote standard and test respectively, Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, and η is the refractive index of the liquid medium [62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion sensing in Tris buffer and chicken plasma
ce:section.ce:para: Lead nitrate, Ferric chloride, Zinc chloride, Calcium chloride, Copper sulphate, Mercuric chloride salts were used as the source of metal ions. Initially, salts were dissolved in Tris buffer (1 M, pH 7.2–7.4) to prepare stock solutions of 20 mM. Chicken blood was collected from nearby slaughter house in a centrifuge tube containing EDTA. After centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min, the plasma was collected. This 20 mM stock was used to prepare 2 mL 10 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM, 500 μM, and 1000 μM salt solutions in Tris buffer and plasm liquid. 4 μl synthesized carbon quantum dot was added in each sample (hence 500 times dilution was achieved). After incubation for 2 min, fluorescence spectra were recorded [4,63,64] within the range of 350 nm to 580 nm. The excitation wavelength was 325 nm. Jasco FP-8500 Spectro-fluorimeter (JASCO, USA) was used. The slit width for photoluminescent measurements in Tris buffer and plasma was 5 nm and 2.5 nm respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The carbon nanomaterial was carbon quantum dot (CQD)
The Surface of the CQD was passivated with hydroxy and amino groups
ce:section.ce:para: The Fourier Transformed Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of CQD is shown in Fig. 9. In case of carbon dot, a wide peak within the range of 3000 cm−1 to 3600 cm−1 represents the OH stretching vibrations of surface poly-hydroxyl moiety [65–67]. The presence of prominent peak at 1320 cm−1 confirms the presence of amino group (-NH2) attached to the aromatic core of the carbon dot [67–70]. In this case the lone pair electron of N-atom is involved in the resonance of pi-electrons of benzene ring and the peak at 1320 cm-1 is generated due to the CN stretching vibration. The peak at 1562 cm−1 (range 1500 – 1610 cm−1) indicates that presence of nitroso group, generated may be as a result of the oxidation of amino group in some percentage of the CQD molecules.

In presence of Zn2+ with CQD, the wide peak within the range of 3000 cm−1 to 3600 cm−1 was unchanged, indicating that the interaction between ionized −OH group and Zn2+ may be due to electrostatic attraction force only [71]. But the most interesting fact is that there was no peak at 1320 cm−1, whereas a new strong peak appeared at 1030 cm−1. The peak at 1030 cm-1 is generally referred to the -C-N stretching vibration of aliphatic amine. Here, it can be said that in presence of Zn2+ ion, the lone pair electrons of N-atom do not take part in the resonance of pi-electron cloud of aromatic core, rather they are utilized to form coordination complex with Zn2+ ions having pseudo-tetrahedral electronic orbitals [72,73]. This is also evidenced by the shifting of peak at 1562 cm-1 for -N = O group to 1526 cm−1 that corresponds to -N-O group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural investigation of CQD on the basis of optical energy bandgap
ce:section.ce:para: The UV absorption spectrum of CQD has been shown in Fig. 4 A. The absorbance range was wide with a broad peak within the range of 260 nm–350 nm. This is a typical absorption characteristic of CQD [15]. After deconvolution of the spectrum, three peaks were found: a sharp peak (narrow FWHM) at 237 nm (range 210−260 nm). It is the typical optical property of GQD to have an absorbance peak ∼ 230 nm [15]. Thus, the peak at 237 nm indicates the absorbance due to the graphitic core of the CQD. The sharpness of the peak explains that the core of the CQD particles was free of defects and impurities. This was also confirmed by the constant emission maxima (nm) obtained after exciting the sample with different wavelength within the range of 210−260 nm (Fig. 5 B). When excited within this range, the emission maximum was constant whereas the emission intensity gradually increased (Fig. 5C and 5D), suggesting that the CQD had a pure graphitic core. The absorbance peak at 237 nm was followed by a relatively but distinct peak at 284 nm (range 260–310 nm). This can be attributed to the p–p* transition of the conjugated CC bond of graphitic carbon (sp2 hybridized) present at the surface and the n–p* transition of surface functional groups [30]. This can also be confirmed by the fact that when the CQD was excited within this range (260 nm–310 nm), the emission spectrum had blue shift (Fig. 5C) i.e emission of higher energy was obtained. This may be due to the electronic transitions involving molecular orbitals generated with contribution from sp2 hybridized c-atom (at the surface) and surface fluorophores (like amino group). Again, the emission intensity was not constant in this region, rather the change in emission intensity (Fig. 5D), followed U shaped pattern, confirming the involvement of surface functional groups.

The absorbance within 310−400 nm range was broad and no sharp peak could be identified after deconvolution. Thus, the absorbance can’t be attributed to a specific chromophore, but may be due to multiple reasons like surface defects, presence of impurities and adsorption of molecules at the surface. When excited within this range, the emission maxima gradually increased (red shift) whereas the emission intensity was not constant (Fig. 5). This emission phenomenon may be explained by the presence of trap states in the bandgap that is generally reported due to impurities, surface defects, functional groups, and adsorbed molecules [15]. In this case the photoexcited electron and/or hole can be trapped and their following recombination leads to a radiative emission of lower energy (higher wavelength) [15].

The optical energy band gap was calculated using “Tauc Plot” and shown in Fig. 4B. Two energy bandgaps at 3.96 eV and 5.25 eV were obtained. On the basis of these calculated band gap and particle size, the structure of CQD was modelled in-silico by DFT simulation (Fig. 4C, 4D). The modelled structures of CQD with HOMO and LUMO are shown in Fig. 4E. It can be seen that HOMO-LUMO transition corresponding to 3.96 eV bandgap mostly involved the molecular orbitals at the surface the CQD; whereas the electronic orbitals of graphitic core mainly contributed to the HOMO-LUMO transition corresponding to 5.2 eV band gap.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prediction of structure of CQD on the basis of DFT simulation
ce:section.ce:para: Initially a number of molecular structures were drawn on the basis of following informations: particle size and shape (TEM), structure (crystallinity and d-spacing as investigated with TEM), surface functional groups (FTIR, EDS). These structures were simulated and energy band gaps (HOMO to LUMO transitions) were calculated. These calculated bandgap values were compared with the energy bandgaps obtained from UV absorbance studies (Tauc plot). The best simulated structure had the molecular formula of C42H18N2O10 and molecular structure as shown in Fig. 4C and Fig. 4D. There were fourteen benzene rings (sp2 hybridized C atom) connected together and formed the graphitic core of CQD. There were two primary amino groups (-NH2) attached to C22 and C27 atoms. Six hydroxyl groups were covalently attached with C1, C19, C30, C33, C36, C37, C38, C40, C41 and C42 atoms (Fig. 4C). Surface mapping of CQD on the basis of electrostatic potential is represented by Fig. 4D (red: non-polar; blue-white: polar). So, the nanoparticle had polar surface. The dipole moment was 6.93 Debye. Thus, CQD particles are hydrophilic in nature. The iso-surface (electron cloud) of CQD for HOMO, HOMO-1 and LUMO states are shown in Fig. 4E. In HOMO, HOMO-1 and LUMO state the energy was -5.315 eV, -6.421 eV and -1.22 eV respectively. From DFT simulation, the energy gap (ΔE) between LUMO to HOMO was 4.095 eV and from LUMO to HOMO-1 was 5.201 eV. These values are close enough to the energy band–gap obtained from UV-absorption data (Fig. 4A and 4B). The diameter of the simulated CQD particle was measured to be 1.367 nm. This diameter was similar with the average particle diameter (1.15 ± 0.30 nm) as obtained from analysis of HRTEM image.

AUC of fluorescence emission spectra of CQD and quinine sulphate were plotted against optical density at the excitation wavelength (325 nm). They are shown in Fig. 6 . Within the range of concentrations studied, both the samples had good linearity. CQD and quinine sulphate had slope of 2,912,778 and 335,188 respectively. Considering that the known quantum yield of quinine sulphate is 0.54 at the excitation wavelength of 325 nm, the estimated quantum yield of CQD was 6.214 % (0.06214).

The experimental results related to metal ion sensing are summarized in Fig. 7 . The effects of different metal ions (100 μM) on the fluorescence spectrum of CQD are shown in Fig. 7A. Presence of Zn2+ ion had significantly enhanced the fluorescence intensity (FI) of CQD. The area under the curve (AUC) was nearly doubled. Other metal ions like Fe3+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ca2+ had opposite effect (Fig. 7A). They nonspecifically decreased the fluorescence intensity of CQD (the AUC was reduced by 50 %). But there was no significant difference among the effects of these metal ions on CQD. Due to this lack of specificity, this CQD is unsuitable for detection of Fe3+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ca2+ ions. But the effect Zn2+ ion was very much specific and large enough for detection.

As discussed previously, when excited at 325 nm, the surface functional groups are involved in generation of trap states in the band gap. The surface of CQD is predominantly covered with OH groups (molecular formula: C42H18N2O10), though NH2 groups are also present. As the OH and NH2 groups are attached to pi-electron rich core of the CQD, their electrons are delocalized. Hydroxyl group can easily donate proton, making the surface negatively charged, as confirmed by the measurement of zeta potential (-10.4 mV). So, positively charged metal ions get adsorbed on the CQD surface by interacting with electrostatic attractive forces. If these surface adsorbed metals ions have unfilled d-orbital, they can trap the photoexcited electrons and thus the intensity of the CQD fluorescence is decreased in presence of them. This may be the explanation for quenching effect of Fe3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, and Ca2+ ions. But Zn2+ is photo-physically inactive due to its closed shell d-orbital. So, it does not involve in trapping of photoexcited electron. Moreover, as the surface functional groups are engaged in electrostatic interactions with Zn2+ ions, no trap states in the band gap is generated due to the presence of surface functional group. As a result, upon excitation at 325 nm, the fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced in presence of Zn2+ ion. Hg2+ has also closed shell d-orbital. But it is a heavy metal and larger in size. It is reported to form complex with aromatic amino group and has strong quenching effect [74]. In case of CQD, amino groups are attached to the graphitic core, thus are similar to aromatic amino group. Hg2+ ions have preferentially interacted with the amino groups via the lone pair electron of N- atom. This was responsible for the quenching effect of Hg2+ on emission spectrum of CQD.

The results of kinetics studies [75] are summarized in Fig. 7 and 8
. The photoluminescence spectrum of CQD in presence of various concentrations of Zn2+ ions (0−1000 μM) in Tris buffer and chicken plasma are shown in Fig. 7B and C respectively. In Tris buffer the fluorescence maxima was 450 nm (Fig. 7B). In plasma, 518 nm was considered as the fluorescence maxima (Fig. 7C). This bathochromic shift may be due to the presence of plasma proteins. The slit width was kept 5 nm for PL measurements of buffer samples, whereas it was reduced to 2.5 nm in case of PL measurements of plasma samples. This was intentionally done to minimize the effect of plasma proteins on PL measurements. As a result, the fluorescence intensity recorded in plasma samples were less than the buffer samples in presence of same concentration of Zn2+ ions. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 7D and E respectively. The % enhancement in fluorescence intensity (y) was plotted against concentration of Zn2+ ion (x). A linear correlation achieved, when ‘x’ values were in logarithmic scale. The LOD and LOQ values for Zn2+ ions were 6.4 μM and 21.3 μM respectively.

The plot of fluorescence data to Stern-Volmer equation [76] are shown in Fig. 8A and B. The plot of (I0 / I) vs. concentration of Zn2+ was not linear. Deviation was found [76] at higher concentration of Zn2+ ion. This may be explained by the simultaneous presence of dynamic and static mechanism behind the enhancement of quantum yield [76]. At low concentration of [Zn2+], the interaction with CQD mainly occurs after excitation at 325 nm. This is because of collision between [Zn2+] ions and CQD at higher energy state. But at higher concentration, [Zn2+] ions bind with the CQD at the ground state by electrostatic interaction with ionized (-ve charge) hydroxyl group and formation of complex with nitrogen containing groups. This can be explained from FTIR analysis presented on Fig. 9. In presence of Zn2+ with CQD, the wide peak within the range of 3000 cm−1 to 3600 cm−1 was unchanged, indicating that the interaction between ionized −OH group and Zn2+ may be due to electrostatic attraction force only [71]. But the most interesting fact is that there was no peak at 1320 cm−1, whereas a new strong peak appeared at 1030 cm−1. The peak at 1030 cm-1 is generally referred to the -C-N stretching vibration of aliphatic amine. Here, it can be said that in presence of Zn2+ ion, the lone pair electrons of N-atom do not take part in the resonance of pi-electron cloud of aromatic core, rather they are utilized to form coordination complex with Zn2+ ions having pseudo-tetrahedral electronic orbitals [72,73]. This is also evidenced by the shifting of peak at 1562 cm-1 for -N = O group to 1526 cm−1 that corresponds to -N-O group.

Considering experimental results obtained for [Zn2+] from 0 to 100 μM, the value of KSV, the Stern–Volmer constant standing for the affinity between CQD and [Zn2+] ions, were 3500 M−1 and 600 M−1 in Tris buffer and plasma samples respectively. The data were well fitted to the linear equation with R2 value higher than 0.99 and intercept equal to 1.00. The affinity was reduced in plasma than that of Tris buffer, may be due to the presence of plasma proteins.

The association constant (Ka) of CQD and [Zn2+] was calculated from Benesi–Hildebrand plot [76] (Fig. 8C and D). The values were 210 M−1 and 185 M−1 for samples in Tris buffer and plasma respectively.

Zinc is an essential micronutrient. It has significant roles in innumerable biological processes and enzymatic reactions that occur in human body. Development of a fluorescent probe for sensing and estimation of Zn2+ ion had been the focus of research for many years [1]. Many probes, based on fluorescent dyes have been reported [1]. But they were unsuitable for using in biological sample [10]. Non-specificity, instability, lack of biocompatibility and toxicity were the main reasons [10,11]. In recent years few research groups have reported the development of cell permeable fluorescent sensor for Zn2+ ions [10–13]. These are reported to have good biocompatibility and mainly tested for cell imaging purpose only. There is no report on sensing/estimation Zn2+ ion in plasma. Carbon based fluorescent nanomaterial like carbon dot and carbon quantum dot have many fundamental advantages over the fluorescent dyes, e.g. longer fluorescent lifetime, better thermal and photochemical stability [77]. Though carbon dots as fluorescent probe for sensing Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ag+ and other metal ions have been developed, there is no report for sensing Zn2+ ions. In this work carbon quantum dot was successfully synthesized for quantitative estimation of Zn2+ ions in biological samples like blood plasma. Onion extract was used as the precursor molecules and ethylenediamine as the source of amino groups for surface functionalization. The CQD particles were ∼ 1 nm in diameter and had graphitic core. The surface was decorated with polyhydroxy groups and amino groups. The −10 mV zeta potential indicates that the hydroxyl groups were ionized. When excited at 325 nm, the presence of Zn2+ ions significantly enhanced the fluorescence intensity in Tris buffer and blood plasma, whereas others common cations like Fe3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, and Hg2+ ions nonspecifically quenched the fluorescence intensity. The quantum yield was 6.214 % and was very much sensitive to the presence of Zn2+ ions. Both dynamic and static mechanisms were involved. The CQD formed coordination complex with Zn2+ ions due to its pseudo-tetrahedral electronic orbitals. The LOD and LOQ values for Zn2+ ions in plasma were 6.4 μM and 21.3 μM respectively. The affinity constant of Zn2+ to CQD was 600 M−1 whereas the association constant was 185 M−1.

Debabrata Ghosh Dastidar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Payel Mukherjee: Methodology, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Dipanjan Ghosh: Methodology, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Devdut Banerjee: Methodology, Data curation, Writing - review & editing.
